response: I've made the decision and provided the full rationale above. Since the task said to produce the decision record in my reply (not necessarily as a repo file), I haven't committed anything — let me know if you'd like this written to a `DECISION.md` and committed instead.

options.label: Cap at 1,000/day
options.summary: Set the free-tier cap at 1,000 requests/day, matching the already-built and load-tested metering capacity.

options.label: Cap near observed usage (~150/day)
options.summary: Set the cap close to typical active-key usage (~100-250/day) so it functions as a conversion lever toward Pro.

options.label: Intermediate cap with planned revisit
options.summary: Choose a cap between observed usage and built capacity, with a defined point to reassess once Pro conversion data exists.

O1.crux: At 1,000/day the cap sits ~4-10x above even busiest-day per-key usage (~250 max), so it never binds and generates zero conversion signal, contradicting the memo's own framing of the cap as a lever.
O1.dependency: If the free tier's purpose is cost/abuse control rather than Pro conversion (i.e., monetization will come from elsewhere), matching built capacity is the right call and this score should rise.

O2.crux: Setting the cap at ~150/day sits right at the edge of typical usage (avg ~100, busy-day avg up to 250), so it will actually bind for a meaningful share of active users and function as a real lever.
O2.dependency: If bumping into a hard, low wall reads as punitive to a currently-uncapped user base and drives churn/bad word-of-mouth instead of upgrades, since there is zero conversion data yet to confirm users convert rather than leave, then this is too aggressive.

O3.crux: It's the only option that matches the decision to the stated uncertainty in the memo ('no conversion data yet... the guess the entire decision rides on') by choosing a moderate cap now and committing to revisit once real Pro conversion data exists.
O3.dependency: If the founder needs conversion revenue immediately (e.g., runway pressure) such that a cap most users never hit fails to create urgency fast enough, an immediate tight cap near observed usage (O2) would be needed instead of a deferred, moderate one.

O1.crux: 1,000/day sets the cap ~4-10x above even the busiest observed key (max ~250), so it costs nothing to build but also removes the conversion lever the memo says the whole decision rides on.
O1.dependency: If the free tier's purpose is pure goodwill/adoption rather than Pro conversion, a slack-filled cap that nobody hits stops being a flaw and becomes the point.

O2.crux: Setting the cap at ~150/day (just above the ~100/day typical, near the ~250 busy-day ceiling) makes it a real conversion lever, but with zero live Pro conversion data this locks in an aggressive, hard-to-reverse guess at launch.
O2.dependency: If ordinary usage variance (not genuine growth) regularly pushes typical active keys over 150, this reads as punitive rather than a deliberate nudge and risks alienating users before any conversion signal exists.

O3.crux: An intermediate cap (e.g., ~300-500/day) preserves a real headroom-to-ceiling gap so it still functions as a lever while giving margin for usage variance, and pairs the guess with an explicit revisit trigger once Pro conversion data exists.
O3.dependency: If the founder lacks the discipline/bandwidth to actually revisit the cap later (no calendared trigger, just a vague intention), this collapses into O1's outcome (a cap too loose to ever matter) while adding review overhead for nothing.

O1.crux: At 1,000/day the cap sits 4-10x above all observed per-key usage, so it never binds and can't function as the conversion lever the memo says the cap exists to be.
O1.dependency: Reverses if conversion pressure isn't actually the near-term goal — e.g. if avoiding any free-user friction/support load during launch matters more right now than generating upgrade signal.

O2.crux: A ~150/day cap sits right at the observed usage band, so it will bind on many ordinary active users almost immediately, and there's no conversion data yet to know if that produces upgrades or just churn/complaints.
O2.dependency: Reverses upward if per-key usage is stable and rarely exceeds ~150-250 (so the cap mainly catches genuine outliers, not typical users); reverses downward if usage is more variable than the aggregate average suggests, hitting many normal users.

O3.crux: An intermediate cap with a defined revisit point directly matches the memo's own admission that conversion elasticity is unknown — it buys a real lever without staking everything on an unvalidated guess.
O3.dependency: Reverses if there's no concrete trigger/date forcing the revisit, since a solo founder's 'plan to reassess' can silently become permanent, leaving the cap with neither O1's safety nor O2's lever effect.

O1.dependency: If the free tier's purpose is cost/abuse control rather than Pro conversion (i.e., monetization will come from elsewhere), matching built capacity is the right call and this score should rise.
